html:
XVII

Supper was over. It had been served in a beautiful old panelled room with an Adam ceiling and fireplace, and the food had been good. Miss Climpson felt braced and ready.

вАЬWeвАЩll sit in my own room, shall we?вАЭ said Miss Booth. вАЬItвАЩs the only really comfortable place. Most of this house is shut up, of course. If youвАЩll excuse me, dear, I will just run up and give Mrs.¬†Wrayburn her supper and make her comfortable, poor thing, and then we can begin. I shanвАЩt be more than half an hour or so.вАЭ

вАЬSheвАЩs quite helpless, I suppose?вАЭ

вАЬYes, quite.вАЭ

вАЬCan she speak?вАЭ

вАЬNot to say speak. She mumbles sometimes, but one canвАЩt make anything of it. ItвАЩs sad, isnвАЩt it, and her so rich. It will be a happy day for her when she passes over.вАЭ

вАЬPoor soul!вАЭ said Miss Climpson.

Her hostess led her into a small, gaily furnished sitting room and left her there among the cretonne covers and the ornaments. Miss Climpson ran her eyes rapidly over the books, which were mostly novels, with the exception of some standard works on Spiritualism, and then turned her attention to the mantelpiece. It was crowded with photographs, as the mantelpieces of nurses usually are. Conspicuous among hospital groups and portraits inscribed вАЬFrom your grateful patient,вАЭ was a cabinet photograph of a gentleman in the dress and moustache of the nineties, standing beside a bicycle, apparently upon a stone balcony in midair with a distant view over a rocky gorge. The frame was silver, heavy and ornate.

вАЬToo young for a father,вАЭ said Miss Climpson, as she turned it over and pulled back the catch of the frame, вАЬeither sweetheart or favourite brother. HвАЩm! вАШMy dearest Lucy from her everloving Harry.вАЩ Not a brother, I fancy. PhotographerвАЩs address, Coventry. Cycle trade, possibly. Now what happened to Harry? Not matrimony, obviously. Death, or infidelity. First-class frame and central position; bunch of hothouse narcissus in a vaseвБ†вАФI think Harry has passed over. What next? Family group? Yes. Names conveniently beneath. Dearest Lucy in a fringe, Papa and Mamma, Tom and Gertrude. Tom and Gertrude are older, but they may be still alive. Papa is a parson. Largeish houseвБ†вАФcountry rectory, perhaps. PhotographerвАЩs address, Maidstone. Wait a minute. HereвАЩs Papa in another group, with a dozen small boys. Schoolmaster, or takes private pupils. Two boys have straw hats with zigzag ribbonsвБ†вАФschool, probably, then. WhatвАЩs that silver cup? Thos. Booth and three other namesвБ†вАФPembroke College Fours 1883. Not an expensive college. Wonder whether Papa objected to Harry on account of the cycle manufacturing connection? That book over there looks like a school prize. It is. Maidstone LadiesвАЩ CollegeвБ†вАФfor distinction in English Literature. Just so. Is she coming back?вБ†вАФNo, false alarm. Young man in khaki, вАШYour loving nephew, G. BoothвАЩвБ†вАФah! TomвАЩs son, I take it. Did he survive, I wonder? YesвБ†вАФshe is coming this time.вАЭ

When the door opened, Miss Climpson was sitting by the fire, deeply engaged in Raymond.

вАЬSo sorry to keep you waiting,вАЭ said Miss Booth, вАЬbut the poor old dear is rather restless this evening. SheвАЩll do now for a couple of hours, but I shall have to go up again later. Shall we begin at once? IвАЩm so eager to try.вАЭ

Miss Climpson readily agreed.

вАЬWe usually use this table,вАЭ said Miss Booth, bringing forward a small, round table of bamboo, with a shelf between its legs. Miss Climpson thought she had never seen a piece of furniture more excellently adapted for the faking of phenomena, and heartily approved of Mrs.¬†CraigвАЩs choice.

вАЬDo we sit in the light?вАЭ she enquired.

вАЬNot in full light,вАЭ said Miss Booth. вАЬMrs.¬†Craig explained to me that the blue rays of daylight or electricity are too hard for the spirits. They shatter the vibrations, you see. So we usually put out the light and sit in the firelight, which is quite bright enough for taking notes. Will you write down, or shall I?вАЭ

вАЬOh I think you had better do it as youвАЩre more accustomed to it,вАЭ said Miss Climpson.

вАЬVery well.вАЭ Miss Booth fetched a pencil and a pad of paper and switched off the light.

вАЬNow we just sit down and place our thumbs and fingertips lightly on the table, near the edge. ItвАЩs better to make a circle, of course, but one canвАЩt do that with two people. And just at first, I think itвАЩs better not to talkвБ†вАФtill a rapport is established, you know. Which side will you sit?вАЭ

вАЬOh, this will do for me,вАЭ said Miss Climpson.

вАЬYou donвАЩt mind the fire on your back?вАЭ

Miss Climpson most certainly did not.

вАЬWell, thatвАЩs a good arrangement, because it helps to screen the rays from the table.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs what I thought,вАЭ said Miss Climpson, truthfully.

They placed thumbs and fingertips on the table and waited.

Ten minutes passed.

вАЬDid you feel any movement?вАЭ whispered Miss Booth.

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬIt sometimes takes a little time.вАЭ

Silence.

вАЬAh! I thought I felt something then.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve got a feeling like pins and needles in my fingers.вАЭ

вАЬSo have I. We shall get something soon.вАЭ

A pause.

вАЬWould you like to rest a little?вАЭ

вАЬMy wrists ache rather.вАЭ

вАЬThey do till you get used to it. ItвАЩs the power coming through them.вАЭ

Miss Climpson lifted her fingers and rubbed each wrist gently. The thin black hooks came quietly down to the edge of the black velvet sleeve.

вАЬI feel sure there is power all about us. I can feel a cold thrill on my spine.вАЭ

вАЬLetвАЩs go on,вАЭ said Miss Climpson. вАЬIвАЩm quite rested now.вАЭ

Silence.

вАЬI feel,вАЭ whispered Miss Climpson, вАЬas though something was gripping the back of my neck.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt move.вАЭ

вАЬAnd my arms have gone dead from the elbow.вАЭ

вАЬHush! so have mine.вАЭ

Miss Climpson might have added that she had a pain in her deltoids, if she had known the name for them. This is not an uncommon result of sitting with the thumbs and fingers on a table without support for the wrist.

вАЬIвАЩm tingling from head to foot,вАЭ said Miss Booth.

At this moment the table gave a violent lurch. Miss Climpson had overestimated the force necessary to move bamboo furniture.

вАЬAh!вАЭ

After a slight pause for recuperation, the table began to move again, but more gently, till it was rocking with a regular seesaw motion. Miss Climpson found that by gently elevating one rather large foot, she could take practically all the weight off her wrist hooks. This was fortunate, as she was doubtful whether their constitution would stand the strain.

вАЬShall we speak to it?вАЭ asked Miss Climpson.

вАЬWait a moment,вАЭ said Miss Booth. вАЬIt wants to go sideways.вАЭ

Miss Climpson was surprised by this statement, which seemed to argue a high degree of imagination, but she obligingly imparted a slight gyratory movement to the table.

вАЬShall we stand up?вАЭ suggested Miss Booth.

This was disconcerting, for it is not easy to work a vibrating table while stooping and standing on one leg. Miss Climpson decided to fall into a trance. She dropped her head on her chest and uttered a slight moan. At the same time she pulled back her hands, releasing the hooks, and the table continued to revolve jerkily, spinning beneath their fingers.

A coal fell from the fire with a crash, sending up a bright jet of flame. Miss Climpson started, and the table ceased spinning and came down with a little thud.

вАЬOh, dear!вАЭ exclaimed Miss Booth, вАЬThe light has dispersed the vibrations. Are you all right, dear?вАЭ

вАЬYes, yes,вАЭ said Miss Climpson, vaguely. вАЬDid anything happen?вАЭ

вАЬThe power was tremendous,вАЭ said Miss Booth. вАЬIвАЩve never felt it so strong.вАЭ

вАЬI think I must have fallen asleep,вАЭ said Miss Climpson.

вАЬYou were entranced,вАЭ said Miss Booth. вАЬThe control was taking possession. Are you very tired, or can you go on?вАЭ

вАЬI feel quite all right,вАЭ said Miss Climpson, вАЬonly a little drowsy.вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩre a wonderfully strong medium,вАЭ said Miss Booth.

Miss Climpson, surreptitiously flexing her ankle, was inclined to agree.

вАЬWeвАЩll put a screen before the fire this time,вАЭ said Miss Booth. вАЬThatвАЩs better. Now!вАЭ

The hands were replaced on the table, which began to rock again almost immediately.

вАЬWe wonвАЩt lose any more time,вАЭ said Miss Booth. She cleared her throat slightly, and addressed the table.

вАЬIs there a spirit here?вАЭ

Crack!

The table ceased moving.

вАЬWill you give me one knock for вАШYesвАЩ and two for вАШNoвАЩ?вАЭ

Crack!

The advantage of this method of interrogation is that it obliges the enquirer to put leading questions.

вАЬAre you the spirit of one who has passed over?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬAre you Fedora?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬAre you one of the spirits who have visited me before?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬAre you friendly to us?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬAre you pleased to see us?вАЭ

вАЬYes. Yes. Yes.вАЭ

вАЬAre you happy?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬAre you here to ask anything for yourself?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬAre you anxious to help us personally?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬAre you speaking on behalf of another spirit?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬDoes he want to speak to my friend?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬTo me, then?вАЭ

вАЬYes. Yes. Yes. Yes.вАЭ (The table rocked violently.)

вАЬIs it the spirit of a woman?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬA man?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

A little gasp.

вАЬIs it the spirit I have been trying to communicate with?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

A pause and a tilting of the table.

вАЬWill you speak to us by means of the alphabet? One knock for A, two for B, and so on?вАЭ

(вАЬBelated caution,вАЭ thought Miss Climpson.)

вАЬCrack!вАЭ

вАЬWhat is your name?вАЭ

Eight taps, and a long indrawn breath.

One tapвБ†вАФ

вАЬHвБ†вАФAвБ†вАФвАЭ

A long succession of taps.

вАЬWas that an R? You go too fast.вАЭ

вАЬCrack!вАЭ

вАЬHвБ†вАФAвБ†вАФRвБ†вАФis that right?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬIs it Harry?вАЭ

вАЬYes, yes, yes.вАЭ

вАЬOh, Harry! At last! How are you? Are you happy?вАЭ

вАЬYesвБ†вАФnoвБ†вАФlonely.вАЭ

вАЬIt wasnвАЩt my fault, Harry.вАЭ

вАЬYes. Weak.вАЭ

вАЬAh, but I had my duty to think of. Remember who came between us.вАЭ

вАЬYes. FвБ†вАФAвБ†вАФTвБ†вАФHвБ†вАФEвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬNo, no, Harry! It was moвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬвБ†вАФAвБ†вАФD!вАЭ concluded the table, triumphantly.

вАЬHow can you speak so unkindly?вАЭ

вАЬLove comes first.вАЭ

вАЬI know that now. But I was only a girl. WonвАЩt you forgive me now?вАЭ

вАЬAll forgiven. Mother forgiven too.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm so glad. What do you do where you are, Harry?вАЭ

вАЬWait. Help. Atone.вАЭ

вАЬHave you any special message for me?вАЭ

вАЬGo to Coventry!вАЭ (Here the table became agitated.)

This message seemed to overwhelm the seeker.

вАЬOh, it really is you, Harry! You havenвАЩt forgotten the dear old joke. Tell meвБ†вАФвАЭ

The table showed great signs of excitement at this point and poured out a volley of unintelligible letters.

вАЬWhat do you want?вАЭ

вАЬGвБ†вАФGвБ†вАФGвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬIt must be somebody else interrupting,вАЭ said Miss Booth. вАЬWho is that, please?вАЭ

вАЬGвБ†вАФEвБ†вАФOвБ†вАФRвБ†вАФGвБ†вАФEвАЭ (very rapidly).

вАЬGeorge? I donвАЩt know any George, except TomвАЩs boy. Has anything happened to him, I wonder.вАЭ

вАЬHa! ha! ha! not George Booth, George Washington.вАЭ

вАЬGeorge Washington?вАЭ

вАЬHa! ha!вАЭ (The table became convulsively agitated, so much so that the medium seemed hardly able to hold it. Miss Booth, who had been noting down the conversation, now put her hands back on the table, which stopped capering and began to rock.)

вАЬWho is here now?вАЭ

вАЬPongo.вАЭ

вАЬWho is Pongo?вАЭ

вАЬYour control.вАЭ

вАЬWho was that talking just now?вАЭ

вАЬBad spirit. Gone now.вАЭ

вАЬIs Harry still there?вАЭ

вАЬGone.вАЭ

вАЬDoes anybody else want to speak?вАЭ

вАЬHelen.вАЭ

вАЬHelen who?вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt you remember? Maidstone.вАЭ

вАЬMaidstone? Oh, do you mean Ellen Pate?вАЭ

вАЬYes, Pate.вАЭ

вАЬFancy that! Good evening, Ellen. How nice to hear from you.вАЭ

вАЬRemember row.вАЭ

вАЬDo you mean the big row in the dormitory?вАЭ

вАЬKate bad girl.вАЭ

вАЬNo, I donвАЩt remember Kate, except Kate Hurley. You donвАЩt mean her, do you?вАЭ

вАЬNaughty Kate. Lights out.вАЭ

вАЬOh, I know what sheвАЩs trying to say. The cakes after lights were out.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs right.вАЭ

вАЬYou still spell badly, Ellen.вАЭ

вАЬMissвБ†вАФMissвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬMississippi? HavenвАЩt you learnt it yet?вАЭ

вАЬFunny.вАЭ

вАЬAre there many of our class where you are?вАЭ

вАЬAlice and Mabel. Send love.вАЭ

вАЬHow sweet of them. Give them my love too.вАЭ

вАЬYes. All love. Flowers. Sunshine.вАЭ

вАЬWhat do youвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬP,вАЭ said the table, impatiently.

вАЬIs that Pongo again?вАЭ

вАЬYes. Tired.вАЭ

вАЬDo you want us to stop?вАЭ

вАЬYes. Another time.вАЭ

вАЬVery well, good night.вАЭ

вАЬGood night.вАЭ

The medium leaned back in her chair with an air of exhaustion which was perfectly justified. It is very tiring to rap out letters of the alphabet, and she was afraid the soapbox was slipping.

Miss Booth turned on the light.

вАЬThat was wonderful!вАЭ said Miss Booth.

вАЬDid you get the answers you wanted?вАЭ

вАЬYes, indeed. DidnвАЩt you hear them?вАЭ

вАЬI didnвАЩt follow it all,вАЭ said Miss Climpson.

вАЬIt is a little difficult, counting, till youвАЩre used to it. You must be dreadfully tired. WeвАЩll stop now and make some tea. Next time perhaps we could use the Ouija. It doesnвАЩt take nearly so long to get the answers with that.вАЭ

Miss Climpson considered this. Certainly it would be less wearisome, but she was not sure of being able to manipulate it.

Miss Booth put the kettle on the fire and glanced at the clock.

вАЬDear me! itвАЩs nearly eleven. How the time has flown! I must run up and see to my old dear. Would you like to read through the questions and answers? I donвАЩt suppose I shall be many minutes.вАЭ

Satisfactory, so far, thought Miss Climpson. Confidence was well established. In a few daysвАЩ time, she would be able to work her plan. But she had nearly tripped up over George. And it was stupid to have said вАЬHelen.вАЭ Nellie would have done for eitherвБ†вАФthere was a Nellie in every school forty-five years ago. But after all, it didnвАЩt much matter what you saidвБ†вАФthe other person was sure to help you out of it. How desperately her legs and arms were aching. Wearily she wondered if she had missed the last bus.

вАЬIвАЩm afraid you have,вАЭ said Miss Booth, when the question was put to her on her return. вАЬBut weвАЩll ring up a taxi. At my expense, of course, dear. I insist, as you were so good in coming all this way, entirely to please me. DonвАЩt you think the communications are too marvellous? Harry would never come beforeвБ†вАФpoor Harry! IвАЩm afraid I was very unkind to him. He married, but you see he has never forgotten me. He lived at Coventry and we used to have a joke about itвБ†вАФthatвАЩs what he meant by saying that. I wonder which Alice and Mabel that was. There was an Alice Gibbons and an Alice RoachвБ†вАФboth such nice girls; I think Mabel must be Mabel Herridge. She married and went out to India years and years ago. I canвАЩt remember her married name and IвАЩve never heard from her since, but she must have passed to the other side. Pongo is a new control. We must ask him who he is. Mrs.¬†CraigвАЩs control is FedoraвБ†вАФshe was a slave girl at the court of Poppaea.вАЭ

вАЬReally!вАЭ said Miss Climpson.

вАЬShe told us her story one night. So romantic. She was thrown to the lions because she was a Christian and refused to have anything to do with Nero.вАЭ

вАЬHow very interesting.вАЭ

вАЬYes, isnвАЩt it? But she doesnвАЩt speak very good English, and itвАЩs sometimes rather hard to understand her. And she sometimes lets the tiresome ones in. Pongo was very quick at getting rid of George Washington. You will come again, wonвАЩt you? Tomorrow night?вАЭ

вАЬCertainly, if you like.вАЭ

вАЬYes, please do. And next time you must ask for a message for yourself.вАЭ

вАЬI will indeed,вАЭ said Miss Climpson. вАЬIt has all been such a revelationвБ†вАФquite wonderful. I never dreamed that I had such a gift.вАЭ

And that was true, also.
